claim: On October 13, 1843, twelve recent German Jewish immigrants — led by Henry Jones (born Heinrich Jonas) and including Isaac Rosenbourg — met at Aaron Sinsheimer's café on New York City's Lower East Side and founded what is today the world's oldest continuously operating Jewish service organization. They originally named it Bundes-Brüder (German for "Brothers of the Covenant"); the Hebrew rendering Bnai Brith / B'nai B'rith (Sons of the Covenant) followed. The founders explicitly modeled the organization on the European fraternal-lodge form, adopted a constitution whose purpose tracked the traditional functions of Jewish societies in Europe — "visiting and attending the sick" and "protecting and assisting the widow and the orphan" — and chose Benevolence, Brotherly Love, and Harmony as the order's motto. Isaac Rosenbourg described the founding motivation as a response to "the deplorable condition of Jews in this, our newly adopted country." From this 1843 nucleus the order grew into a national, then international, network with lodges across the U.S. and Europe; began publishing The Menorah as its official monthly organ in 1886 (Benjamin Franklin Peixotto, editor); produced a sequence of nationally visible Jewish-American advocates, including Simon Wolf (longtime B'nai B'rith president and emissary to multiple U.S. presidents — see [[1888-wolf-influence-speech-001]]); and in 1913 founded the Anti-Defamation League in Chicago in response to the Leo Frank case (see [[y1913-frank-adl]]). B'nai B'rith is the parent organization for every later episode that names "the ADL" or "B'nai B'rith" on this timeline.

sources.label: B'nai B'rith International — "History of Service" (the organization's own institutional history page). States the founding date, place, and Henry Jones as leader of twelve German Jewish immigrants.
sources.quote: The first meeting of B'nai B'rith took place on Oct. 13, 1843, at Sinsheimer's Café on New York City's Lower East Side.

sources.label: The Menorah: A Monthly Magazine. Official organ of the B'ne B'rith — vol. 1 (1886), edited by Benjamin Franklin Peixotto. Primary period publication; demonstrates that the order was producing its own press by the mid-1880s and provides contemporaneous self-description.
Encyclopedia of Judaism — "B'nai B'rith" (Encyclopedia.com). Secondary reference with the founding date, place, Sinsheimer's café, Henry Jones, Isaac Rosenbourg, and the original German name.
sources.quote: True to their German heritage, the founders originally named the organization Bundes Bruder (Sons of the Covenant)

sources.label: Wikipedia — "B'nai B'rith." Compiles the founding facts (date, place, twelve founders, Henry Jones, Bundes-Brüder, motto, founding-constitution language) with footnoted citations to Deborah Dash Moore's B'nai B'rith and the Challenge of Ethnic Leadership (SUNY, 1981) and the Shengold Jewish Encyclopedia.
sources.quote: B'nai B'rith was founded in Aaron Sinsheimer's café in New York City's Lower East Side on 13 October 1843, by 12 recent German Jewish immigrants led by Henry Jones.

sources.label: Deborah Dash Moore, B'nai B'rith and the Challenge of Ethnic Leadership (State University of New York Press, Albany, 1981) — the canonical academic-history monograph for the order's founding and 19th–20th-century institutional trajectory. Cited by Wikipedia footnote [6] for the founding facts above.

Edward E. Grusd, B'nai B'rith: The Story of a Covenant (Appleton-Century, New York, 1966) — institutional history written for the order's 125th anniversary; standard biographical source for the founders and the early lodge structure.

notes: The cleanest primary source for the very first 1843 meeting is the order's own self-narration, which has been institutionally maintained without contradiction for 180 years and is corroborated by the academic monograph (Moore 1981) and the institutional history (Grusd 1966). No surviving 1843 minute-book has been digitized publicly; The Menorah (1886+) is the earliest digitized in-house publication on archive.org. This record exists primarily as the parent-organization anchor for the existing 1913 ADL record and the 1888 Wolf record, so that downstream entries that name 'B'nai B'rith' or 'the ADL' have an explicit 1843-founding receipt at the head of the timeline rather than a reference to an unanchored organization. Themes are deliberately restricted to the existing taxonomy (adl, journalism); the order's later financial / international / political activities should ride on the records that document them, not on this founding-pure record.
